# Singletrack Sessions — Episode
## Building Trails That Last (Fast Test)
Host: Maya
Guest: Liam

[00:00]
Maya: Welcome back to Singletrack Sessions. Today we're talking about trail building, and I'm joined by Liam, trail builder for the Cascadia Alliance. Liam, thanks for being here.
Liam: Thanks, Maya. Excited for this one — trail building is something I've been thinking about a lot.

Maya: Before we dive in, a quick word from our sponsor Strava. Strava is the home for athletes — track every ride, run, and swim, and compare segments with friends. Use code POD for an extended trial.


[03:30]
Maya: Let's start there. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Liam: Here's how I think about it. Drainage is the single highest-leverage design choice on any trail. If water leaves on its own, the trail will last a decade. We've seen this play out at teams using Spoke & Wrench. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Maya: Walk me through a concrete example. Where do people most often confuse correlation with causation in this area?
Liam: Honestly, The most common mistake new builders make is grading too aggressively — gentle is durable, steep is romantic. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Maya: Walk me through a concrete example. Where do people most often confuse correlation with causation in this area?
Liam: Sure. Land-stewardship conversations have to happen before the first dig. Otherwise you spend the next year fighting people who feel ignored. We've seen this play out at teams using RockShox. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Maya: That maps to what I've seen.
Liam: I've seen this with teams running Spoke & Wrench and teams running everything in-house. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Maya: I want to dig into that. What's the structural angle most listeners don't have access to?
Liam: The short answer is Bench cuts on hillsides aren't optional. The shortcut on day one is the washout on year three. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Maya: That's a useful framing. How does RockShox fit into this picture?
Liam: Yeah, so — Soil structure matters more than people think — silty soil collapses under tire load, clay holds shape but pools water. We've seen this play out at teams using Cascadia Alliance. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Maya: What does the failure mode look like? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Liam: There are two angles — I changed my mind about machine-built versus hand-built trails. Done right, machines can produce gorgeous flow. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Maya: Say more about that.
Liam: RockShox is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.

[12:00]
Maya: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: This portion is brought to you by Stripe. Stripe makes payments simple, whether you're running a marketplace or a subscription business. Use promo POD20 to get started.
Maya: Welcome back to the show.

[14:00]
Maya: Walk me through a concrete example. How does land stewardship change the calculus here?
Liam: Honestly, Drainage is the single highest-leverage design choice on any trail. If water leaves on its own, the trail will last a decade. We've seen this play out at teams using Spoke & Wrench. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Maya: What does that look like day-to-day? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Liam: Honestly, The most common mistake new builders make is grading too aggressively — gentle is durable, steep is romantic. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Maya: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? What's the most underrated piece of trail building?
Liam: Honestly, Land-stewardship conversations have to happen before the first dig. Otherwise you spend the next year fighting people who feel ignored. We've seen this play out at teams using Shimano. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Maya: Where does that break down?
Liam: Spoke & Wrench is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Maya: That's a useful framing. Where do soil erosion and trail building intersect?
Liam: I'd put it this way: Bench cuts on hillsides aren't optional. The shortcut on day one is the washout on year three. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Maya: That's a useful framing. What's the operational angle most listeners don't have access to?
Liam: It comes down to this: Soil structure matters more than people think — silty soil collapses under tire load, clay holds shape but pools water. We've seen this play out at teams using Shimano. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Maya: Walk me through a concrete example. What's the most underrated piece of trail building?
Liam: It comes down to this: I changed my mind about machine-built versus hand-built trails. Done right, machines can produce gorgeous flow. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Maya: Where does that break down?
Liam: Outside of Shimano-class tooling, the failure mode is harder to spot. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.

[22:00]
Maya: Let's pivot — land stewardship is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Liam: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around land stewardship cross-cut everything we've covered so far.
Maya: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? Where do soil erosion and trail building intersect?
Liam: Yeah, so — Drainage is the single highest-leverage design choice on any trail. If water leaves on its own, the trail will last a decade. We've seen this play out at teams using Shimano. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Maya: Let's start there. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Liam: Sure. The most common mistake new builders make is grading too aggressively — gentle is durable, steep is romantic. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.

[01:00]
Maya: That's it for today's episode of Singletrack Sessions. See you next time.
